bio.name: Charles Crabtree
bio.title: Senior Lecturer (Associate Professor)
bio.institution: Monash University, School of Social Sciences
bio.secondary: K-CLUB Professor, University College, Korea University
bio.summary: Political scientist studying intergroup relations and conflict, focusing on how social boundaries like race, ethnicity, class, disability, and nationality are constructed and contested. Uses AI and computational methods to measure and reduce discrimination.
bio.background: Grew up in poverty in a blended biethnic family. Lived in trailer parks and public housing. Worked across politics, research, journalism, education, design, and data science before academia. PhD from University of Michigan. Former Assistant Professor at Dartmouth, Visiting Assistant Professor at Stanford APARC.
bio.interests_outside_work: Family, travel, yoga, photography, cooking, basketball. Roots for North Melbourne in AFL.
bio.editorial_roles: Associate Editor at Australian Journal of Political Science. Secretary of the Australian Society for Quantitative Political Science. Editorial boards: Asian Journal of Comparative Politics, Japanese Journal of Political Science.

research_areas.description: Documenting and explaining discrimination against groups defined by disability, ethnicity, gender, nativity, race, and religion. Current focus on class-based discrimination.
Understanding attitudes of individuals facing food and housing needs, and what the public thinks of them. Housed in the Fundamental Needs Lab at needslab.org.
Using computational text analysis, machine learning, and large language models to study social and political phenomena at scale. Exploring whether AI can reduce prejudice.
Research on discrimination, gender politics, and political behavior in Japan and the broader Asia-Pacific.
Research on public support for human rights, naming and shaming, and rights in authoritarian contexts.
Developing and refining methodological tools for measuring discriminatory behavior, including correspondence audits, validated name lists, and survey design.

key_publications.title: Can AI Help Reduce Prejudice?
key_publications.topic: AI-powered personalized persuasion to reduce prejudice

key_publications.title: Geo-political Rivalry and Anti-Immigrant Sentiment
key_publications.topic: Conjoint experiment in 22 countries on immigration attitudes

key_publications.title: Are Americans Less Likely to Reply to Emails from Black People?
key_publications.topic: Large-scale field experiment on racial discrimination in email responses

key_publications.title: Patient Traits Shape Health-Care Stakeholders' Choices
key_publications.topic: How patient characteristics affect life-saving care allocation

key_publications.title: Testing the Causal Impact of Social Media Reduction Around the Globe
key_publications.topic: Global experiment on reducing social media use

key_publications.title: Evade and Deceive? Citizen Responses to Surveillance
key_publications.topic: How citizens respond to surveillance in authoritarian contexts

key_publications.title: When Do Private Actors Engage in Censorship?
key_publications.topic: Correspondence experiment with Russian private media firms

key_publications.title: Validated Names for Experimental Studies on Race and Ethnicity

current: Political Theory in a Changing World (Monash University)
AI and Experimental Methods (IPSA-NUS Summer School)
past_highlights: Experiments in Politics (Dartmouth) — students published papers
Baltic LEAP foreign study program — 'life-changing' student reviews
teaching.philosophy: Views teaching as ongoing conversations in and outside the classroom. Teaches statistical reasoning, research design, democratization, and discrimination. Strives to be approachable, encouraging, and emotionally supportive.

University of Michigan Center for Japanese Studies
